problem_statement: Current development practices inadequately address security risks unique to AI systems in critical infrastructure, and high-level AI governance frameworks (e.g., NIST AI RMF) lack the concrete implementation roadmaps engineering teams need in regulated environments.
magnitude_evidence: Recent comprehensive analysis consolidates '43 AI security frameworks and 777 identified risks'; catalog development analyzed '127 documented AI security incidents from critical infrastructure sectors'
insufficiency_evidence: Frameworks like NIST AI RMF, IEC 62443 and NERC CIP 'were developed before widespread AI adoption and lack specific controls for AI-related threats'; baseline audit-artifact completeness was only 62% manual coverage vs 100% automated with the framework
notes: Off-topic drift: the paper concerns securing AI/ML systems (models) in critical infrastructure, not human oversight of AI-generated code; 'oversight' here refers to governance of AI model risk/security rather than review of AI-authored code.
